description: Review flashcards on chromosomal aberrations and clinical genetics

cards.text: What is the difference between euploidy and aneuploidy?
cards.answer: Euploidy: changes involving complete sets of chromosomes (triploidy 3n, tetraploidy 4n). Aneuploidy: gain or loss of individual chromosomes (trisomy, monosomy)/euploidy is whole sets; aneuploidy is individual chromosomes

cards.text: What are the clinical features of Down syndrome (Trisomy 21)?
cards.answer: Intellectual disability, flat facial profile, upslanting palpebral fissures, single palmar crease, congenital heart defects (AV canal), hypotonia/intellectual disability, facial features, heart defects, hypotonia

tip.tip: Most common autosomal trisomy compatible with survival

cards.text: What is the karyotype and key features of Klinefelter syndrome?
cards.answer: 47,XXY — tall stature, gynecomastia, small testes, infertility, mild intellectual impairment/47XXY tall gynecomastia small testes infertility

tip.tip: Most common sex chromosome aneuploidy in males

cards.text: What is Edward syndrome and its karyotype?
cards.answer: Trisomy 18 — severe intellectual disability, rocker-bottom feet, overlapping fingers, congenital heart defects; most die within first year/trisomy 18

tip.tip: Clenched fist with overlapping fingers is characteristic

cards.text: What is the difference between a mosaic and a chimera?
cards.answer: Mosaic: two or more genetically different cell lines from a single zygote (mitotic error). Chimera: different cell lines from different zygotes (fusion or twin transfer)/mosaic from one zygote; chimera from two zygotes

tip.tip: Mosaics are far more common clinically

cards.text: What is FISH and what is it used for in genetics?
cards.answer: Fluorescence in situ hybridisation — uses fluorescent DNA probes to detect specific chromosomal regions; rapid diagnosis of aneuploidies and microdeletions/fluorescent probes to detect specific DNA sequences on chromosomes

tip.tip: Faster than karyotyping; can detect microdeletions invisible on karyotype

cards.text: What is Patau syndrome and its karyotype?
cards.answer: Trisomy 13 — holoprosencephaly, cleft lip and palate, polydactyly, congenital heart defects; most die within first month/trisomy 13

tip.tip: Most severe of the viable autosomal trisomies

cards.text: What are the types of structural chromosomal aberrations?
cards.answer: Deletion, duplication, inversion (paracentric and pericentric), translocation (reciprocal and Robertsonian), ring chromosome, isochromosome/deletion duplication inversion translocation ring isochromosome

tip.tip: Balanced rearrangements may be phenotypically normal but cause problems in offspring
